name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَرْسَلَ ٱلرِّيَـٰحَ فَتُثِيرُ سَحَابًا فَسُقْنَـٰهُ إِلَىٰ بَلَدٍ مَّيِّتٍ فَأَحْيَيْنَا بِهِ ٱلْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِهَا ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ ٱلنُّشُورُ (٩)
```

```english
It is Allah who sends the winds and they raise a cloud; then We drive it toward a dead land and with it revive the earth after its death. Likewise will be the resurrection [of the dead]. (9)
```

```arabic
مَن كَانَ يُرِيدُ ٱلْعِزَّةَ فَلِلَّهِ ٱلْعِزَّةُ جَمِيعًا ۚ إِلَيْهِ يَصْعَدُ ٱلْكَلِمُ ٱلطَّيِّبُ وَٱلْعَمَلُ ٱلصَّـٰلِحُ يَرْفَعُهُۥ ۚ وَٱلَّذِينَ يَمْكُرُونَ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ شَدِيدٌ ۖ وَمَكْرُ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُوَ يَبُورُ (١٠)
```

```english
Whoever seeks honour [should know that] honour entirely belongs to Allah. To Him ascends the good word, and He elevates righteous conduct; as for those who devise evil schemes, there is a severe punishment for them, and their plotting shall come to naught. (10)
```

```arabic
وَٱللَّهُ خَلَقَكُم مِّن تُرَابٍ ثُمَّ مِن نُّطْفَةٍ ثُمَّ جَعَلَكُمْ أَزْوَٰجًا ۚ وَمَا تَحْمِلُ مِنْ أُنثَىٰ وَلَا تَضَعُ إِلَّا بِعِلْمِهِۦ ۚ وَمَا يُعَمَّرُ مِن مُّعَمَّرٍ وَلَا يُنقَصُ مِنْ عُمُرِهِۦٓ إِلَّا فِى كِتَـٰبٍ ۚ إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ يَسِيرٌ (١١)
```

```english
Allah created you from dust, then from a drop of [seminal] fluid, then He made you mates. No female conceives or delivers except with His knowledge, and no elderly person advances in years, nor is anything diminished of his life, but it is [recorded] in a Book. That is indeed easy for Allah. (11)
```

```arabic
وَمَا يَسْتَوِى ٱلْبَحْرَانِ هَـٰذَا عَذْبٌ فُرَاتٌ سَآئِغٌ شَرَابُهُۥ وَهَـٰذَا مِلْحٌ أُجَاجٌ ۖ وَمِن كُلٍّ تَأْكُلُونَ لَحْمًا طَرِيًّا وَتَسْتَخْرِجُونَ حِلْيَةً تَلْبَسُونَهَا ۖ وَتَرَى ٱلْفُلْكَ فِيهِ مَوَاخِرَ لِتَبْتَغُوا۟ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ (١٢)
```

```english
Not alike are the two seas: this one sweet and agreeable, pleasant to drink, and that one briny and bitter, and from each you eat fresh meat and obtain ornaments, which you wear. And you see the ships plowing through them, that you may seek of His bounty, and so that you may give thanks. (12)
```

```arabic
يُولِجُ ٱلَّيْلَ فِى ٱلنَّهَارِ وَيُولِجُ ٱلنَّهَارَ فِى ٱلَّيْلِ وَسَخَّرَ ٱلشَّمْسَ وَٱلْقَمَرَ كُلٌّ يَجْرِى لِأَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى ۚ ذَٰلِكُمُ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّكُمْ لَهُ ٱلْمُلْكُ ۚ وَٱلَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِهِۦ مَا يَمْلِكُونَ مِن قِطْمِيرٍ (١٣)
```

```english
He makes the night pass into the day and makes the day pass into the night, and He has disposed the sun and the moon, each moving for a specified term. That is Allah, your Lord; to Him belongs all sovereignty. As for those whom you invoke besides Him, they do not control so much as the husk of a date stone. (13)
```

```arabic
إِن تَدْعُوهُمْ لَا يَسْمَعُوا۟ دُعَآءَكُمْ وَلَوْ سَمِعُوا۟ مَا ٱسْتَجَابُوا۟ لَكُمْ ۖ وَيَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ يَكْفُرُونَ بِشِرْكِكُمْ ۚ وَلَا يُنَبِّئُكَ مِثْلُ خَبِيرٍ (١٤)
```

```english
If you invoke them they will not hear your invocation, and even if they heard they cannot respond to you, and on the Day of Resurrection they will forswear your polytheism, and none can inform you like the One who is all-aware. (14)
```

## (Commentary)

These are arguments for His oneness in His divinity (الألوهية), following a series of celestial and terrestrial blessings that man enjoys, for which there is no creator or regulator (مدبر) except Allah, the Glorified. Within them is also an allusion to the Resurrection (البعث).

His saying, the Exalted: **وَاللهُ الَّذِي أَرْسَلَ الرِّياحَ فَتُثِيرُ سَحاباً فَسُقْناهُ إِلى بَلَدٍ مَيِّتٍ** — *It is Allah who sends the winds and they stir up a cloud, then We drive it to a dead land* [35:9], etc.

The focus in this context is on the actual occurrence of rainfall and the subsequent growth of vegetation. For this reason, He said: **اللهُ الَّذِي أَرْسَلَ الرِّياحَ** (Allah is He who sent the winds), using the past tense. This differs from the phrasing in Surah al-Rum: **اللهُ الَّذِي يُرْسِلُ الرِّياحَ فَتُثِيرُ سَحاباً** — *It is Allah who sends the winds and they stir up a cloud* [30:48], where the present tense is used.

His saying: **فَتُثِيرُ سَحاباً** (and they stir up a cloud) is conjoined to **أَرْسَلَ** (sent). The pronoun refers to the winds. The use of the present tense (المضارع) is to depict the past state (حكاية الحال الماضية). Stirring (الإثارة) is the causative form (إفعال) from the root "the dust stirred" (ثار الغبار), meaning it spread and rose up.

His saying: **فَسُقْناهُ إِلى بَلَدٍ مَيِّتٍ** (then We drive it to a dead land) means to a land with no vegetation. **فَأَحْيَيْنا بِهِ الْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِها** — *then We revive the earth with it after its death* [35:9]; that is, We caused plants to grow in it after they were non-existent. Attributing life (الإحياء) to the earth, though metaphorical (مجازية), is literal (حقيقية) when attributed to the plants. The functions of plants—such as nutrition, growth, reproduction, and related processes—are vital activities (أعمال حيوية) emanating from the principle of life (أصل الحياة).

Therefore, He likened the Resurrection (البعث) and the reviving of the dead after their death to the reviving of the earth after its death—meaning the growth of plants after their cessation and dormancy in winter—saying: **كَذلِكَ النُّشُورُ** — *likewise is the resurrection* [35:9]. The resurrection (النشور) is the spreading out of the dead on the Day of Judgment after their revival and emergence from the graves.

In His saying: **فَسُقْناهُ إِلى بَلَدٍ مَيِّتٍ** (then We drive it to a dead land), there is a transition (التفات) from the third person (الغيبة) to the first person plural (التكلم مع الغير). In His saying: **وَاللهُ الَّذِي أَرْسَلَ** (And it is Allah who sent), He is described in the third person, while in **فَسُقْناهُ** (then We drive it), He is described in the first person plural. Perhaps the subtle point (النكتة) here is that when He said: **وَاللهُ الَّذِي أَرْسَلَ الرِّياحَ**, He took the attribute of the third person, and the act of "sending" followed it, as the act of an absent one is itself absent. Then, when He said: **فَتُثِيرُ سَحاباً** in the manner of depicting a past state, the listener becomes as if he is seeing the act and witnessing the winds as they stir the clouds and spread them in the atmosphere. Thus, it is as if he sees the One who sends the winds, because witnessing the act is almost inseparable from witnessing the Agent (الفاعل). When the Exalted appeared in the attribute of presence (الحضور), He changed the style of His speech from the third person to the first person, choosing the plural form to indicate majesty (العظمة).

His saying: **فَأَحْيَيْنا بِهِ الْأَرْضَ** (then We revived the earth with it), and He did not say "then We revived it" (فأحييناه), though it would have sufficed. Similarly, His saying: **بَعْدَ مَوْتِها** (after its death), though it could have been omitted based on what preceded it, was stated to adopt an explicit declaration (صريح القول) about which there is no doubt.

His saying, the Exalted: **مَنْ كانَ يُرِيدُ الْعِزَّةَ فَلِلَّهِ الْعِزَّةُ جَمِيعاً** — *Whoever seeks honour—to Allah belongs all honour* [35:10]. Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: "Honour (العزة) is a state that prevents a person from being overcome, derived from their saying: 'hard ground' (أرض عزاز), meaning solid." The Exalted said: **أَيَبْتَغُونَ عِنْدَهُمُ الْعِزَّةَ فَإِنَّ الْعِزَّةُ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعاً** — *Do they seek honour with them? Indeed, all honour belongs to Allah* [4:139]. (End of quote).

Thus, solidity (الصلابة) is the root meaning of honour (العزة). It was then expanded so that "the Mighty" (العزيز) is used for one who subdues and is not subdued, as in His saying: **يا أَيُّهَا الْعَزِيزُ مَسَّنا** — *O Emir! Distress has befallen us* [12:88]. Likewise, honour (العزة) can mean victory (الغلبة), as the Exalted said: **وَعَزَّنِي فِي الْخِطابِ** — *and he has got the better of me in speech* [38:23]. Honour (العزة) can also mean scarcity and being difficult to attain, as the Exalted said: **وَإِنَّهُ لَكِتابٌ عَزِيزٌ** — *and indeed it is an august Book* [41:41]. It can mean absolute difficulty, as the Exalted said: **عَزِيزٌ عَلَيْهِ ما عَنِتُّمْ** — *grievous to him is your distress* [9:128]. It can also mean pride (الأنفة) and zeal (الحمية), as the Exalted said: **بَلِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فِي عِزَّةٍ وَشِقاقٍ** — *Rather the faithless are in [the grip of] pride and defiance* [38:2], among other meanings.

Furthermore, honour (العزة) in the sense of a thing being subduing and not subdued, or victorious and not defeated, belongs in its true sense exclusively to Allah, the Mighty and Majestic. For anyone other than the Exalted is poor in his essence (ذاته) and lowly in himself, possessing nothing for himself unless Allah has mercy on him and grants him some honour, as He did for the believers. The Exalted said: **وَلِلَّهِ الْعِزَّةُ وَلِرَسُولِهِ وَلِرَسُولِهِ وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ** — *Yet all might belongs to Allah and His Apostle and the faithful* [63:8].

From this, it becomes clear that His saying: **مَنْ كانَ يُرِيدُ الْعِزَّةَ فَلِلَّهِ الْعِزَّةُ جَمِيعاً** is not intended to state that honour is so exclusive to Allah that no one else can attain it, or that whoever seeks it has sought the impossible. Rather, the meaning is: whoever wants honour, let him seek it from the Exalted, because all honour belongs to Him and is not found inherently (بالذات) with anyone else.

Thus, placing His saying: **فَلِلَّهِ الْعِزَّةُ جَمِيعاً** as the response to the condition (جزاء الشرط) is an instance of placing the cause (السبب) in the position of the effect (المسبب), which is seeking it from Him—that is, acquiring it from Him through servitude (العبودية), which is only achieved through faith and righteous deeds (العمل الصالح).

His saying, the Exalted: **إِلَيْهِ يَصْعَدُ الْكَلِمُ الطَّيِّبُ وَالْعَمَلُ الصَّالِحُ يَرْفَعُهُ** — *To Him ascends the good word, and He elevates righteous conduct* [35:10]. The word (الكلم) is—as has been said—a collective generic noun (اسم جنس جمعي) that can be treated as masculine or feminine. It is said in *al-Majma'*: "*al-Kalim* (الكلم) is the plural of *kalimah* (كلمة). It is said: 'this is *kalim*' (هذا كلم) and 'these are *kalim*' (هذه كلم), so it is treated as both masculine and feminine. Every plural that differs from its singular only by the letter *ha* (الهاء) may be treated as masculine or feminine." (End of quote).

In any case, what is meant by "the word" (الكلم) is that which conveys a complete linguistic meaning, as evidenced by its description as "good" (الطيب). The goodness of a word is its suitability to the soul of its listener and speaker, such that the soul expands because of it, finds pleasure in it, and is perfected by it. This only occurs when it conveys a true meaning (معنى حقا) containing the soul's felicity and success.

From this, it appears that what is meant is not the mere phonetic utterance, but rather the good meaning it carries. Thus, it refers to true beliefs (الاعتقادات الحقة) through which man finds felicity by submitting to them and basing his actions upon them. The most certain of these is the word of monotheism (كلمة التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — to which all other true beliefs return. This is what is encompassed by His saying, the Exalted: **أَلَمْ تَرَ كَيْفَ ضَرَبَ اللهُ مَثَلاً كَلِمَةً طَيِّبةً كَشَجَرَةٍ طَيِّبَةٍ أَصْلُها ثابِتٌ وَفَرْعُها فِي السَّماءِ تُؤْتِي أُكُلَها كُلَّ حِينٍ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّها** — *Have you not regarded how Allah draws a parable? A good word is like a good tree: its roots are steady and its branches are in the sky. It gives its fruit every season by the leave of its Lord* [14:24-25]. Calling a belief a "saying" or a "word" is a common practice among them.

The ascent (صعود) of the good word to Him, the Exalted, is its drawing near to Him by way of elevation, for He is the Most High (العلي الأعلى), the Exalted in Rank (رفيع الدرجات). Since belief is an attribute subsisting in the believer, its drawing near to Him is the drawing near of the believer to Him. Some have interpreted the ascent of the good word as His acceptance (القبول) of it, which is one of the implications of the meaning.

Furthermore, when belief and faith are true and sincere within the soul, they are confirmed by action and not belied by it; that is, actions proceed in accordance with them. Action is one of the branches of knowledge and its inseparable effects. Whenever an action is repeated, the belief increases in firmness and clarity, and its influence grows stronger. Thus, righteous deeds (العمل الصالح)—which are deeds worthy of acceptance, characterized by the offering of servitude and sincerity for His noble sake—assist the true belief in producing its effect, which is the ascent to Him. This is what is referred to by "elevating" (الرفع); thus, righteous conduct elevates the good word.

It has become clear from what has passed the meaning of His saying: **إِلَيْهِ يَصْعَدُ الْكَلِمُ الطَّيِّبُ وَالْعَمَلُ الصَّالِحُ يَرْفَعُهُ**. The pronoun in **إِلَيْهِ** (to Him) refers to Allah, the Glorified. The "good word" refers to true belief, such as monotheism (التوحيد). Its "ascent" is its drawing near to Him. "Righteous conduct" refers to what is in accordance with and suitable to the true belief. The agent (الفاعل) in **يَرْفَعُهُ** (elevates it) is a hidden pronoun referring to "righteous conduct," and the object pronoun refers to the "good word."

Others have different views regarding this verse:

It has been said that the ascent of the good word means its acceptance and the granting of reward for it, as previously mentioned. It has also been said that it refers to the ascent of the angels with the recorded faith and acts of obedience to Allah, the Glorified. It has been said that it means their ascent with it to the heavens, so the ascent to the heavens is metaphorically called an ascent to Allah.

It has been said that the agent of **يَرْفَعُهُ** is a pronoun returning to the "good word," and the object pronoun refers to "righteous conduct." The meaning would be that the good word elevates righteous conduct; i.e., righteous conduct is of no benefit unless it proceeds from monotheism. It has also been said that the agent of **يَرْفَعُهُ** is a hidden pronoun returning to Him, the Exalted, meaning: Allah elevates righteous conduct.

All of these views are not without some far-fetchedness (بعد), and the meaning that first comes to mind is what we previously stated.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَالَّذِينَ يَمْكُرُونَ السَّيِّئاتِ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ شَدِيدٌ وَمَكْرُ أُولئِكَ هُوَ يَبُورُ** — *As for those who plot evil schemes, there is a severe punishment for them, and their plotting will come to naught* [35:10]. They mentioned that "evil" (السَّيِّئاتِ) is an adjective standing in place of an omitted noun, which is "plots" (المكرات). The use of the demonstrative pronoun (اسم الإشارة) instead of a simple pronoun in **مَكْرُ أُولئِكَ** (the plotting of those) is to indicate that they are distinct and not mixed with others. The meaning is: those who plot evil plots shall have a severe punishment, and the plotting of those plotters shall perish (يبور) and be destroyed, leaving no living trace that contains their felicity or honour.

It has become clear that "evil" refers to the types of plots and ruses that the polytheists (المشركون) take as means to gain honour. The verse is general (مطلقة). It has been said that it refers to the plots that the Quraysh devised against the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in the Assembly Hall (دار الندوة) and elsewhere—to imprison, expel, or kill him. Allah turned their plot back upon them, brought them out to Badr, killed them, and cast them into the well (القليب), thus combining for them imprisonment, expulsion, and death. This is a fine interpretation, but the verse remains general.

The connection between the end of the verse—namely, His saying: **إِلَيْهِ يَصْعَدُ** to the end—and His saying: **مَنْ كانَ يُرِيدُ الْعِزَّةَ فَلِلَّهِ الْعِزَّةُ جَمِيعاً** is that the polytheists used to seek honour through their gods, as the Exalted said: **وَاتَّخَذُوا مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ آلِهَةً لِيَكُونُوا لَهُمْ عِزًّا** — *They have taken gods besides Allah that they may be a [source of] might for them* [19:81]. So Allah, the Glorified, called them to Himself while they were seeking honour, by reminding them that all honour belongs to Allah. The Exalted explained this by stating that monotheism (توحيده) ascends to Him and righteous conduct elevates it, so man acquires honour from the source of honour by drawing near to Him. As for those who plot every evil plot to acquire honour, they shall have a severe punishment, and their plotting is futile and perishing; it does not ascend to any place nor does it earn them any honour.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَاللهُ خَلَقَكُمْ مِنْ تُرابٍ ثُمَّ مِنْ نُطْفَةٍ ثُمَّ جَعَلَكُمْ أَزْواجاً** — *Allah created you from dust, then from a drop of fluid, then He made you pairs* [35:11], etc. The Exalted points to the creation of man, beginning his creation from dust, which is the remote origin (المبدأ البعيد) to which creation traces back, then from a drop of fluid (نطفة), which is the proximate origin (مبدأ قريب) to which creation is attached.

It has been said that "creating them from dust" refers to the creation of their father Adam from dust, as a thing is attributed to its root. It has been said that it refers to the creation of Adam himself. It has also been said that it refers to creating them collectively (إجماليا) from dust within the creation of Adam from dust, while the detailed creation (الخلق التفصيلي) is from a drop of fluid, as He said: **ثُمَّ مِنْ نُطْفَةٍ**.

The difference between these three views is that in the first, the attribution of creation from dust to them is by way of intellectual metaphor (المجاز العقلي). In the second, what is meant by "creating them" is the creation of Adam, and there is no metaphor in the attribution. In the third, what is meant is the creation of every individual from dust in reality without metaphor, except that it is a collective creation, not a detailed one; this distinguishes it from the first view we mentioned.

The first view can be supported by His saying, the Exalted: **خَلَقَ الْإِنْسانَ مِنْ صَلْصالٍ كَالْفَخَّارِ** — *He created man from dry clay like in pottery* [55:14]. The second by something like His saying: **وَبَدأَ خَلْقَ الْإِنْسانِ مِنْ طِينٍ ثُمَّ جَعَلَ نَسْلَهُ مِنْ سُلالَةٍ مِنْ ماءٍ مَهِينٍ** — *and He began the creation of man from clay. Then He made his progeny from an extract of a base fluid* [32:7-8]. The third by His saying: **وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْناكُمْ ثُمَّ صَوَّرْناكُمْ ثُمَّ قُلْنا لِلْمَلائِكَةِ اسْجُدُوا لِآدَمَ** — *Certainly We created you, then We formed you, then We said to the angels, ‘Prostrate before Adam’* [7:11]. Each view has its merit.

His saying: **ثُمَّ جَعَلَكُمْ أَزْواجاً** (then He made you pairs) means males and females. It has been said: He ordained marriage (الزوجية) among you and paired some of you with others, but this is as you see (weak). It has also been said: He made you into types and nations, which is like the preceding view.

His saying: **وَما تَحْمِلُ مِنْ أُنْثى وَلا تَضَعُ إِلَّا بِعِلْمِهِ** — *No female conceives or delivers except with His knowledge* [35:11]. The word "any" (من) is extra (زائدة) to emphasize the negation. The preposition "with" (الباء) in **بِعِلْمِهِ** (with His knowledge) denotes accompaniment (المصاحبة), and it is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the acts of conceiving and delivering. The meaning is: no female conceives or delivers except that His knowledge accompanies her conceiving and delivering. Some mentioned it is a circumstantial qualifier for the agent (الفاعل), but that it being a qualifier for the acts of conceiving and delivering, or for their objects—the conceived and the delivered—is contrary to the apparent meaning, which is rejected.

His saying: **وَما يُعَمَّرُ مِنْ مُعَمَّرٍ وَلا يُنْقَصُ مِنْ عُمُرِهِ إِلَّا فِي كِتابٍ** — *and no aged person is given [a long] life, nor is any part of his life curtailed, but it is in a Book* [35:11]. That is, no one's life is extended or increased so that he becomes aged (معمرا), nor is any part of his life—meaning anyone's life—curtailed, except that it is in a Book.

His saying: **وَما يُعَمَّرُ مِنْ مُعَمَّرٍ** is like the saying: **إِنِّي أَرانِي أَعْصِرُ خَمْراً** — *I saw myself pressing wine* [12:36]. The word "aged" (معمر) is placed in the position of the pro-agent (نائب الفاعل), which is "anyone," with the consideration that after the act of aging is applied to him, he becomes aged; otherwise, "aging the aged" would be meaningless.

His saying: **وَلا يُنْقَصُ مِنْ عُمُرِهِ** (nor is any part of his life curtailed), the pronoun in **عُمُرِهِ** (his life) refers to "aged" (معمر) in consideration of its omitted described noun, which is "anyone." The meaning is: nor is the life of anyone curtailed. Otherwise, curtailing the life of one who is assumed to be aged would be a contradiction that violates the assumption.

His saying: **إِلاَّ فِي كِتابٍ** (but it is in a Book) refers to the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ) which is not subject to change. It is written therein that so-and-so shall have his life increased by such an amount for such a reason, and so-and-so shall have his life curtailed by such an amount for such a reason. As for the Book of Effacement and Affirmation (كتاب المحو والإثبات), it is the locus of change. The context of the verse conveys the description of fixed knowledge (العلم الثابت). Regarding His saying: **وَما يُعَمَّرُ مِنْ مُعَمَّرٍ وَلا يُنْقَصُ مِنْ عُمُرِهِ**, they have other weak views that are not worth addressing.

His saying: **إِنَّ ذلِكَ عَلَى اللهِ يَسِيرٌ** — *That is indeed easy for Allah* [35:11]. This is a justification and confirmation of the description of man's creation and the manner of his origination and preservation mentioned in the verse. The meaning is that this precise and firm regulation (التدبير), which dominates universal and particular events and places everything in its proper place, is easy for Allah because He is Allah, the All-Knowing, the All-Powerful, who encompasses everything with His knowledge and power. Thus, He, the Exalted, is the Lord of man just as He is the Lord of everything.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَما يَسْتَوِي الْبَحْرانِ هذا عَذْبٌ فُراتٌ سائِغٌ شَرابُهُ وَهذا مِلْحٌ أُجاجٌ** — *The two seas are not equal: this one is sweet, thirst-quenching, and pleasant to drink, and that one is salt and bitter* [35:12], to the end of the verse. It is said: "sweet" (العذب) water is that which is good; "thirst-quenching" (الفرات) is water that breaks thirst or is cold, as in *al-Majma'*; "pleasant" (السائغ) is that which descends easily down the throat due to its sweetness; and "bitter" (الأجاج) is that which burns due to its saltiness or is acrid.

His saying: **وَمِنْ كُلٍّ تَأْكُلُونَ لَحْماً طَرِيًّا وَتَسْتَخْرِجُونَ حِلْيَةً تَلْبَسُونَها** — *From each you eat fresh meat and extract ornaments that you wear* [35:12]. "Fresh meat" (اللحم الطري) is tender and new, referring to the meat of fish, or fish and birds of the sea. The "ornaments" (الحلية) extracted from the sea are pearls, coral, and shells. The Exalted said: **يَخْرُجُ مِنْهُمَا اللُّؤْلُؤُ وَالْمَرْجانُ** — *From them both emerge the pearl and the coral* [55:22].

In the verse is a parable (تمثيل) for the believer and the disbeliever (الكافر) using the sweet and salt seas. It clarifies that the believer and the disbeliever are not equal in primordial perfection (الكمال الفطري), even if they share most human characteristics and their effects. The believer remains upon his original primordial nature (الفطرة), through which he attains the felicity of eternal life. The disbeliever has deviated from it, becoming shrouded in what the human nature does not find pleasant, and he will be punished for his deeds. Their likeness is that of two seas differing in sweetness and saltiness; they are different in terms of remaining upon the original nature of water—which is sweetness—or departing from it through saltiness, even if they share some beneficial effects. From each of them you eat fresh meat—which is the meat of fish and birds caught from the sea—and you extract ornaments that you wear, such as pearls, coral, and shells.

The apparent meaning of the verse is that the extracted ornaments are common to both the sweet sea and the salt sea. However, a group of commentators found this problematic, arguing that pearls and coral are only extracted from the salt sea, not the sweet one. They have provided various answers to this:

First, that the verse is intended to state the shared benefit of the two seas in general, even if some benefits are specific. It is as if it were said: "From each you benefit and profit, just as you eat fresh meat from both, and you extract ornaments from the salt sea that you wear, and you see the ships sailing through it."

Second, that He likened the believer and the disbeliever to the sweet and bitter waters, then He favored the bitter water over the disbeliever, in that there is some benefit in the bitter water while there is no benefit in the existence of the disbeliever. Thus, the verse follows the method of His saying: **ثُمَّ قَسَتْ قُلُوبُكُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ ذلِكَ فَهِيَ كَالْحِجارَةِ أَوْ أَشَدُّ قَسْوَةً** — *Then your hearts hardened after that; so they are like stones, or even harder in hardness* [2:74], then He said: **وَإِنَّ مِنَ الْحِجارَةِ لَما يَتَفَجَّرُ مِنْهُ الْأَنْهارُ** — *For indeed there are some stones from which rivers gush forth* [2:74].

Third, that His saying: **وَتَسْتَخْرِجُونَ حِلْيَةً تَلْبَسُونَها** is part of the completion of the parable, meaning that although the two seas share some benefits, they differ in what is intended essentially (بالذات), because one of them has been mixed with something that removed it from the purity of its nature. Similarly, the believer and the disbeliever, though they may occasionally agree in some noble traits like courage and generosity, are different in what is fundamental, because one remains upon the purity of the original nature while the other does not.

Fourth, that there is nothing to prevent pearls from emerging from sweet waters even if we have not seen it; thus, the objection that ornaments are exclusive to salt water is rejected.

Fifth, rejecting the basis of the claim itself—that the verse **وَما يَسْتَوِي الْبَحْرانِ** etc., is a parable for the believer and the disbeliever. Rather, it occurs in the context of enumerating blessings to prove Lordship (الربوبية), like His previous saying: **وَاللهُ الَّذِي أَرْسَلَ الرِّياحَ** and His subsequent saying: **يُولِجُ اللَّيْلَ فِي النَّهارِ** etc. Thus, the verse is intended to explain the blessing of the sea, its variation in sweetness and saltiness, and the shared and specific benefits within them.

This view is supported by the fact that the counterpart of this verse in Surah al-Nahl occurs in the context of verses enumerating the blessings of Allah, the Glorified, which is His saying: **وَهُوَ الَّذِي سَخَّرَ الْبَحْرَ لِتَأْكُلُوا مِنْهُ لَحْماً طَرِيًّا وَتَسْتَخْرِجُوا مِنْهُ حِلْيَةً تَلْبَسُونَها وَتَرَى الْفُلْكَ مَواخِرَ فِيهِ وَلِتَبْتَغُوا مِنْ فَضْلِهِ وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ** — *It is He who disposed the sea [for your benefit] that you may eat fresh meat from it, and extract from it ornaments which you wear, and you see the ships plowing through it, and that you may seek of His bounty, and that you may give thanks* [16:14].

The truth is that the basis of the objection is misplaced, and that the two seas share in the presence of ornaments within them, as is mentioned and explained in books researching these matters (1).

His saying, the Exalted: **وَتَرَى الْفُلْكَ فِيهِ مَواخِرَ لِتَبْتَغُوا مِنْ فَضْلِهِ وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ** — *and you see the ships plowing through it, that you may seek of His bounty and that you may give thanks* [35:12]. The pronoun in **فِيهِ** (in it) refers to the sea. **مواخر** (plowing) is the plural of *makhirah*, from *makhr*, meaning to cleave. The ship is called *makhirah* because it cleaves the water with its prow.

It is said: the singular pronoun of address was used in **تَرَى** (you see), unlike the preceding and succeeding addresses, because the address is to everyone from whom seeing is possible, not just those who benefit from the two seas.

His saying: **لِتَبْتَغُوا مِنْ فَضْلِهِ وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ** means the ships cleave the sea by His disposal so that you may seek His gift, which is sustenance (الرزق), and in the hope that you will thank Allah, the Glorified. It has been previously mentioned that the hope (الترجي) conveyed by "perhaps" (لعل) in His speech, the Exalted, resides in the context, not in the Speaker.

It has been noted that in this verse it says: **وَتَرَى الْفُلْكَ فِيهِ مَواخِرَ لِتَبْتَغُوا مِنْ فَضْلِهِ**, while in Surah al-Nahl it says: **وَتَرَى الْفُلْكَ مَواخِرَ فِيهِ وَلِتَبْتَغُوا مِنْ فَضْلِهِ**. The two verses differ in the placement of **فِيهِ** (in it) before or after **مَواخِرَ** (plowing), and in the use of the conjunction "and" (الواو) before **لِتَبْتَغُوا** (that you may seek).

Perhaps the subtle point is that the verse in al-Nahl begins with the word "disposed" (سخر), so it is intended to explain the manner of disposal. It is more appropriate there to place **فِيهِ** later so it relates to "plowing" and points to the cleaving of the sea, thus explicitly stating the disposal. This is unlike the case here. Furthermore, disposal has many goals, one of which is seeking bounty; thus, it was more appropriate there to conjoin **لِتَبْتَغُوا** to an omitted part to indicate that the goal is not limited to seeking bounty. This is unlike the case here, where the purpose is to explain that He is the Provider and Regulator so that the deniers—whose denial was previously mentioned—might be deterred from their denial. For this, it suffices to state their seeking of bounty as a goal without the need for a conjunction.

And Allah knows best.

It is said in *Ruh al-Ma'ani* regarding this: "What appears to me in this is that the verse in al-Nahl was intended to enumerate blessings, as indicated by what precedes and follows it, and the conclusion of the verses with His saying: **وَإِنْ تَعُدُّوا نِعْمَةَ اللهِ لا تُحْصُوها** — *If you enumerate Allah’s blessings, you will not be able to count them* [16:18]. Thus, the most important thing there was to present what constitutes a blessing, which is the ships' plowing of the water. This is unlike the case here, which was mentioned as a digression or a completion of the parable, as you learned just now. Thus, **فِيهِ** was presented here to signal that this was not the primary intent. The emphasis there required saying **وَلِتَبْتَغُوا** with the 'and', while the difference here required omitting the 'and' in His saying **لِتَبْتَغُوا**." (End of quote).

His saying, the Exalted: **يُولِجُ اللَّيْلَ فِي النَّهارِ وَيُولِجُ النَّهارَ فِي اللَّيْلِ وَسَخَّرَ الشَّمْسَ وَالْقَمَرَ كُلٌّ يَجْرِي لِأَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى** — *He makes the night pass into the day and makes the day pass into the night, and He has disposed the sun and the moon, each moving for a specified term* [35:13], etc. Making the night pass into the day is the shortening of the day by the lengthening of the night, and making the day pass into the night is the shortening of the night by the lengthening of the day. The two phrases refer to the continuous variation of the night and day in length and shortness throughout the days of the year due to the changing of the days. Therefore, He expressed it with **يُولِجُ** (He makes pass into), which indicates continuous change, unlike the movement of the sun and the moon, which is constant in its state. Thus, He expressed it with: **وَسَخَّرَ الشَّمْسَ وَالْقَمَرَ كُلٌّ يَجْرِي لِأَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى**. The focus is on the apparent form, with some simplification.

His saying: **ذلِكُمُ اللهُ رَبُّكُمْ** — *That is Allah, your Lord* [35:13] is like a conclusion to what preceded; i.e., if the matter of your creation and regulation on land and sea, and in earth and sky, is attributed to Him and regulated by His regulation, then that is Allah, your Lord, who owns you and regulates your affairs.

His saying: **لَهُ الْمُلْكُ** — *to Him belongs all sovereignty* [35:13] is deduced from what precedes it and serves as a preamble for what follows: **وَالَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ ما يَمْلِكُونَ مِنْ قِطْمِيرٍ**.

His saying: **وَالَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ ما يَمْلِكُونَ مِنْ قِطْمِيرٍ** — *and those whom you invoke besides Him do not possess [even] the skin of a date-stone* [35:13]. The "skin of a date-stone" (القطمير), according to al-Raghib, is the mark on the head of the date-stone, used as a metaphor for something trivial. In *al-Majma'*, it is the wrapping of the date-stone, and it is said: the speck in the belly of the date-stone. (End of quote). In any case, the speech is a hyperbole in negating the very basis of ownership. What is meant by "those whom you invoke besides Allah" are their gods whom they used to invoke, including idols and their lords (أربابها).

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنْ تَدْعُوهُمْ لا يَسْمَعُوا دُعاءَكُمْ وَلَوْ سَمِعُوا مَا اسْتَجابُوا لَكُمْ** — *If you invoke them, they will not hear your invocation, and even if they heard, they would not respond to you* [35:14], etc.

This is an explanation and confirmation of what preceded: **وَالَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ ما يَمْلِكُونَ مِنْ قِطْمِيرٍ**. That is, the proof that they possess nothing is that if you invoke them, they do not hear your invocation, because idols are inanimate objects with no consciousness or sense, and the lords of the idols—like angels and holy humans—are too occupied to attend to that. Furthermore, they do not possess hearing of their own accord; they only hear if He makes them hear.

His saying: **وَلَوْ سَمِعُوا مَا اسْتَجابُوا لَكُمْ** (and even if they heard, they would not respond to you) because they have no power to respond in word or deed. As for the idols, this is obvious. As for the lords of the idols, their power is from Allah, the Glorified, and Allah will never permit anyone to respond to one who invokes him with divinity. The Exalted said: **لَنْ يَسْتَنْكِفَ الْمَسِيحُ أَنْ يَكُونَ عَبْداً لِلَّهِ وَلَا الْمَلائِكَةُ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ وَمَنْ يَسْتَنْكِفْ عَنْ عِبادَتِهِ وَيَسْتَكْبِرْ فَسَيَحْشُرُهُمْ إِلَيْهِ جَمِيعاً** — *The Messiah would never disdain to be a servant of Allah, nor would the microbes brought near [to Him]. Whoever disdains His worship and is arrogant, He will gather them all toward Him* [4:172].

His saying: **وَيَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ يَكْفُرُونَ بِشِرْكِكُمْ** — *and on the Day of Resurrection they will disown your polytheism* [35:14]; that is, they will return your worship to you and disassociate themselves from you instead of being intercessors for you. **إِذْ تَبَرَّأَ الَّذِينَ اتُّبِعُوا مِنَ الَّذِينَ اتَّبَعُوا** — *when those who were followed will disown those who followed [them]* [2:166].

Thus, the verse regarding the negation of response and the partners' disavowal on the Day of Resurrection is similar in meaning to His saying: **وَمَنْ أَضَلُّ مِمَّنْ يَدْعُوا مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ مَنْ لا يَسْتَجِيبُ لَهُ إِلى يَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ وَهُمْ عَنْ دُعائِهِمْ غافِلُونَ وَإِذا حُشِرَ النَّاسُ كانُوا لَهُمْ أَعْداءً وَكانُوا بِعِبادَتِهِمْ كافِرِينَ** — *Who is more astray than him who invokes besides Allah such [entities] as will not respond to him until the Day of Resurrection, and who are oblivious of their invocation? When the people are gathered [on the Day of Resurrection], they will be their enemies and they will disown their worship* [46:5-6].

His saying: **وَلا يُنَبِّئُكَ مِثْلُ خَبِيرٍ** — *and none can inform you like the One who is all-aware* [35:14]. That is, no informer can inform you of the reality of the matter like an informer who is all-aware (خبير). This is a specific address to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) after turning away from addressing them due to their lack of understanding of the true explanation. Or it is a general address in the form of a specific one, addressed to any listener, as in His saying: **وَتَرَى الْفُلْكَ فِيهِ مَواخِرَ** in the previous verse, and His saying: **وَتَرَى الشَّمْسَ إِذا طَلَعَتْ** — *You may see the sun, when it rises* [18:17], and His saying: **وَتَحْسَبُهُمْ أَيْقاظاً وَهُمْ رُقُودٌ** — *You would have taken them for awake, while they were asleep* [18:18].

## (Narrative Discussion)

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding the saying of the Exalted: **كَذلِكَ النُّشُورُ** (likewise is the resurrection), it is narrated from my father, from Ibn Abi 'Umayr, from Jamil ibn Darraj, from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: **إذا أراد الله أن يبعث الخلق ـ أمطر السماء على الأرض أربعين صباحا ـ فاجتمعت الأوصال ونبتت اللحوم** — "When Allah intends to resurrect the creation, He will cause the sky to rain upon the earth for forty mornings, then the joints will come together and the flesh will grow."

I say: There are several other narrations with this same meaning.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, it is recorded by al-Tayalisi, Ahmad, 'Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in *al-Asma' wa al-Sifat*, from Abu Razin al-'Uqayli, who said: "I said: 'O Messenger of Allah, how does Allah revive the dead?' He said: **أما مررت بأرض مجدبة ـ ثم مررت بها مخصبة تهتز خضراء؟** — 'Have you not passed by a barren land, then passed by it again fertile, vibrating with greenery?' He said: 'Yes.' He said: **كذلك يحيي الله الموتى وكذلك النشور** — 'Thus does Allah revive the dead, and likewise is the resurrection.'"

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), he said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: **إن لكل قول مصداقا من عمل يصدقه أو يكذبه ـ فإذا قال ابن آدم ـ وصدق قوله بعمله رفع قوله بعمله إلى الله ، وإذا قال وخالف عمله قوله ـ رد قوله على عمله الخبيث وهوى به في النار** — 'Indeed, for every saying there is a confirmation from action that either verifies it or belies it. So when the son of Adam speaks and verifies his saying with his action, his saying is elevated by his action to Allah. But if he speaks and his action contradicts his saying, his saying is rejected upon his evil action and he falls with it into the Fire.'"

In *al-Tawhid*, with its chain of narration from Zayd ibn 'Ali, from his father (peace be upon him), in a hadith he said: **وإن لله تبارك وتعالى بقاعا في سماواته ـ فمن عرج به إلى بقعة منها فقد عرج به إليه. ألا تسمع الله عز وجل يقول : «تَعْرُجُ الْمَلائِكَةُ وَالرُّوحُ إِلَيْهِ» ويقول في قصة عيسى بن مريم عليه‌السلام «بَلْ رَفَعَهُ اللهُ» ويقول عز وجل : « إِلَيْهِ يَصْعَدُ الْكَلِمُ الطَّيِّبُ وَالْعَمَلُ الصَّالِحُ يَرْفَعُهُ »** — "Indeed, for Allah, Blessed and Exalted, there are places in His heavens; so whoever is ascended to a place among them has been ascended to Him. Do you not hear Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, saying: 'The angels and the Spirit ascend to Him' [70:4], and He says in the story of Jesus son of Mary (peace be upon him): 'Rather Allah raised him up' [4:158], and the Mighty and Majestic says: 'To Him ascends the good word, and He elevates righteous conduct' [35:10]."

I say: A similar narration is found in *al-Faqih*.

In *Nahj al-Balagha*: **ولو لا إقرارهن له بالربوبية ـ وإذعانهن له بالطواعية لما جعلهن موضعا لعرشه ولا مسكنا لملائكته ـ ولا مصعدا للكلم الطيب والعمل الصالح من خلقه** — "Had it not been for their (the heavens') acknowledgment of His Lordship and their submission to Him in obedience, He would not have made them the place for His Throne, nor a dwelling for His angels, nor a place of ascent for the good word and righteous conduct from His creation."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): regarding the saying of the Exalted: **وَما يَسْتَوِي الْبَحْرانِ ـ هذا عَذْبٌ فُراتٌ سائِغٌ شَرابُهُ وَهذا مِلْحٌ أُجاجٌ**, he said: **الأجاج المر** — "*al-Ujaj* (الأجاج) is the bitter."

In the same source: regarding His saying: **وَالَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ ـ ما يَمْلِكُونَ مِنْ قِطْمِيرٍ**, he said: **الجلدة الرقيقة التي على ظهر النوى** — "The thin skin that is on the back of the date-stone."
